title: What Is the Millennium?
summary: A clear explanation of premillennial, amillennial, and postmillennial views, then Revelation 20 in its own sequence.

sections.title: Three common views
body: Christians have often grouped millennium views into three broad families. Premillennialism teaches that Christ returns before the thousand years. Amillennialism usually reads the millennium symbolically as the present reign of Christ and His saints. Postmillennialism expects the gospel to transform the world in such a way that a golden age precedes Christ's return.

Each view tries to honor some part of Scripture: Christ's victory, the present reign of grace, the spread of the gospel, or the hope of final restoration. But Revelation 20 must be allowed to speak in its own sequence. The chapter follows the visible defeat of beast powers in Revelation 19 and precedes the final judgment and the New Jerusalem.

sections.title: Christ comes before the thousand years
body: Revelation's order is significant. The rider on the white horse appears in chapter 19, the beast and false prophet are defeated, and the rest are slain. Then chapter 20 shows Satan bound for a thousand years. This places the millennium after the public coming of Christ and the destruction of the living wicked, not before the second coming.

The binding of Satan is not a gradual improvement of world conditions. He is bound because he has no living nations left to deceive. Revelation later says that when the thousand years are finished, he is released and goes out to deceive the nations again. His binding is therefore tied to circumstances, not a conversion of his character.

sections.title: Two resurrections
body: Revelation 20 distinguishes the first resurrection from what happens after the thousand years. Those who share in the first resurrection are blessed and holy; the second death has no power over them. The rest of the dead do not live again until the thousand years are finished. This is one of the clearest structural features of the chapter.

The first resurrection belongs to the righteous at Christ's coming. The wicked dead remain dead during the thousand years. At the end, they are raised for final judgment. This reading fits Jesus' teaching that there will be a resurrection of life and a resurrection of condemnation, and it preserves Revelation's movement from deliverance to review to final execution of judgment.

sections.title: Judgment during the thousand years
body: During the millennium, the saints reign with Christ and judgment is given to them. This does not mean they decide God's character or improve His verdicts. It means the redeemed participate in a transparent review of God's judgments. Scripture repeatedly shows that God wants His ways understood and vindicated before the universe.

This helps explain why final destruction does not happen instantly at the second coming. God allows the redeemed to see the justice of His dealings. The millennium is not a second probation for the wicked. It is a solemn interval in which God's government is shown to be righteous before sin is finally eradicated.

sections.title: The end of sin
body: At the end of the thousand years, Satan is released, deceives the nations, and gathers them against the beloved city. This final uprising reveals that rebellion has not been healed by time, evidence, or circumstance. Even after the millennium, Satan's spirit remains unchanged. The wicked stand outside the city and choose rebellion again.

Then fire comes from God and devours them. The lake of fire is the second death. After this, John sees a new heaven and a new earth. The millennium therefore serves the larger purpose of final closure. Sin is exposed, judged, and ended so that God may dwell with His people without death, sorrow, crying, or pain.
The millennium matters because Revelation shows God ending sin transparently, not carelessly. The redeemed are delivered, the wicked are judged, Satan is exposed, and God's justice is seen.
The hope of Revelation 20 is not an improved version of the present world. It is the complete end of rebellion and the arrival of God's restored creation.
